What will U.S. tariffs look like on Friday? With USTR still not having finalized its Section 301 “forced labor” tariffs, let’s unpack the options:

1. The 10% “Section 122” tariffs that President Trump declared on Feb. 20, hours after the Supreme Court ruled that his “IEEPA” tariffs were illegal, expires just after midnight on Friday morning (July 24).

2. My base case scenario has been and remains that USTR will finalize its Section 301 tariffs on "forced labor" in the coming days and impose those tariffs starting on Friday to prevent a gap in Trump tariffs. This means that most U.S. trading partners will face either a 10% rate or a 12.5% rate, depending on the country. As with the IEEPA tariffs and the Section 122 tariffs, the Section 301 tariffs will generally be on top of underlying MFN tariffs, though I expect USTR to stack the MFN and Section 301 rates so as to avoid combined tariff rates exceeding the rates the U.S. agreed to in the Trump “ART” trade deals. (Also, about half of imported products are slated to be exempt from the Section 301 tariffs).

3. In this base case scenario, a large number of countries, such as Uganda, Paraguay, Kenya, Ghana, Panama, etc. will see their Trump tariff rates drop to 0, because the “forced labor” section 301 does not cover them, and I do not see a legal authority to continue imposing tariffs on those countries. The Section 301 covers countries that make up 99% of U.S. imports, so the economic impact of the 0 rate to the U.S. will be small. That said, a number of these smaller countries are AGOA members and some of their products, such as textiles, could benefit from the fact there will likely at least temporarily have a lower Trump tariff rate than most larger countries.

4. USTR still has not finalized its separate “industrial overcapacity” 301. As a result, on July 24, most countries will face 10% or 12.5% “forced labor” Section 301 tariffs, *rates that are in most cases lower than Trump’s original IEEPA tariff rates.* While I do expect USTR to use the industrial overcapacity 301 and other tools to eventually restore most of the IEEPA tariff rates, it seems we will get less-than-IEEPA rates for a bit longer, even after the Section 122 expires.

5. If USTR does not finish the “forced labor” 301 in time, or if it wants to cover the small countries, Trump could always try to declare a second 122, to continue tariffs at 10%. The statutory language of Section 122 pretty clearly legally forecloses this, but Trump might figure it is better to litigate the matter and pay refunds in a year rather than see tariffs fall off some countries. (Plus, there is maybe a 10% chance he’d win in court).

6. In any event, quite interested to see how this plays out over the next week.

* Brazil will face a separate 25% Section 301 tariff starting July 22 pursuant to a separate Section 301 investigation finalized this past week on alleged Brazilian unfair trade practices.

@PPGMacro are you saying China's GDP numbers don't add up?  :)

Caixin: "Chinese households are spending the smallest share of their incomes since the depths of the pandemic. While per capita disposable income rose 4.2% year-on-year in real terms in the first half of the year, consumer spending lagged far behind with just a 2.7% increase."

Caixin notes that while households consumed 68-69% of their income before the pandemic, they are now consuming less than 65%, even as banks are under pressure to expand consumer lending. This probably reflects rising worries about job prospects.

For all the policy talk about boosting the role of consumption in driving growth, in other words, the economy is moving in the opposite direction. Not only is disposable household income rising more slowly in real terms than than GDP (4.7% in the first six months of 2026), but households are also saving a higher share of their income.

As the household share of GDP declines, there are only two ways to balance this. One way is for the investment share to rise, which hasn't been happening recently and, given how few productive investment opportunities remain in China, is unlikely to happen without an even greater surge in debt.

The other way, of course, is for the share of net exports in driving GDP to rise.
https://t.co/gZAxC5CwjM

Good SCMP article that illustrates one dilemma facing the Chinese economy: "While official manufacturing data for June showed continued expansion, electronics exporters in the region – one of China’s biggest industrial areas and a bellwether for the...
https://t.co/BpOd8ZnEUV

Reuters: "The surge in bad consumer debt is mainly the result of ​last year's relaxed credit issuance to meet government consumption targets, said bankers with knowledge of the matter."

The only sustainable way to raise the role of consumption in driving economic growth is to raise the share of GDP that households are directly or indirectly able to retain. Temporary measures, like issuing consumption vouchers, forcing banks to expand consumer lending, or encouraging shopping holidays, only shift consumption, often from the future to the present.

If we are certain that future growth will take off, and drive a consumption surge with it, these temporary measures can be a good way to stabilize growth. But if growth instead slows, today's various "consumption boosting" strategies will only exacerbate the slowdown.

In the five‑step loop I teach, “diagnose” is the pivot point that turns a vague problem into a concrete lever for improvement. When you stop at step 2 and only name the symptom—say, “our portfolio underperformed this quarter”—you’re still not seeing the underlying cause. Sit with the discomfort long enough to ask: Is the issue a missing skill in our research team, a mis‑aligned incentive structure, or perhaps an external shock we didn’t anticipate? Use radical transparency and thoughtful disagreement to pull in other perspectives; remember that the best answer often lies outside your own head. Once you have a clear root cause, you can design a solution that hits the real source rather than just patching the surface. If the diagnosis reveals something you can’t fix yourself, escalate it or seek help—that’s part of the “best answer” principle. The key is to be specific about what you’re diagnosing, then iterate until the fix sticks. What kind of problems do you typically spot but hesitate to dig into deeply, and how might you bring in others to help you see the root cause?

This is concerning. For the first time, a Chinese model Kimi K3 has taken #1 on the Frontend Code Arena and is scoring at or near the frontier on other benchmarks.  

Meanwhile America is tying itself in knots: politicians and bureaucrats are banning new data centers, piling on state regulations, and pushing for new federal agencies to pre-approve frontier models. 

This is how you lose the AI race. The rest of the world won’t play by our rules if we bog ourselves down. Permissionless innovation is how America won the internet and became the technological envy of the world. We can do it again with AI -- while addressing risks in a targeted way -- or we’ll watch our lead evaporate.
